questions.prompt: Adding a new optional field to a response — backward compatible?

choices.text: Backward compatible. Most JSON parsers ignore unknown fields by default; consumers that don't expect the new field simply don't read it. Their code continues to work. Exception: strictly-validated parsers (some statically-typed languages with explicit schema validation set to 'fail on extra fields') might error — these are rare in practice and a sign of bad client design. Adding fields is the safest API evolution path: ship new functionality additively without major version bumps. Removing fields, renaming, changing types — all breaking. Tightening validation — breaking (rejects previously-valid requests). Adding required request fields — breaking (old clients without them now fail). The rule: additive changes are safe; subtractive or restrictive changes are breaking. Most API evolution can be expressed additively if you plan ahead; major version bumps should be rare.

questions.explanation: Backward compatible — consumers ignore unknown fields. Additive changes are safe; subtractive/restrictive are breaking.

questions.prompt: You want to rename `user_name` → `username` in responses. How without breaking consumers?

choices.text: Ship BOTH for a deprecation period: `{"user_name": "Aaron", "username": "Aaron"}`. New clients use `username`; old clients still see `user_name`. Mark `user_name` as deprecated in docs + add a `Deprecation` response header noting the future removal date. After the deprecation window (months to years depending on your customer base), include only `username` in the next major version bump. Same pattern for renaming endpoints (old endpoint redirects to new for a while, then 410 Gone), changing parameter names (accept both, prefer new). Direct rename breaks anyone reading the old name; the dual-field period gives consumers time to migrate without coordination. Stripe-style date versioning solves this differently: old account versions keep the old name; new account versions get the new name; the server maintains the mapping. Either pattern requires deliberate maintenance of multiple shapes; the cost is real but predictable. Without it, consumer integrations break overnight.

questions.explanation: Ship both fields for a deprecation period; warn via headers + docs; remove in next major.
